item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it is Rights Managed by Science Source (as indicated here), making it non-compliant. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…a variety of art forms, such as performance art, photography, art, film footage, poetry, and prose pieces.

So one of the art forms was art? Probably should be edited out of the list, but I won't touch in case there was some other intent behind that. Also, the punctuation after "forms" should not be a comma. Semi-colon? I don't actually remember the grammar rule for such cases.

But I did fix the "twenty-five (30)" error in the playback log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this was the one that made me register my own account. after reading the current version, and comparing it to the old revisions (including the original), it's…so well done. what a great inversion of the approach to "sentient beings in a recording" that we've seen so many times in other SCPs. +1, one of my favorites on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminds me of some scips that have already been mentioned but… it goes in its unique and marvelous way. I also like the idea of "what if a loving couple was given the means and the time to experience everything there is to do? Would their love survive?." I guess not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Issues with this
– hide block

SCP-1614-1 and -2 spend the former portion of Playback 009

There is no Playback 009. How are we supposed to know what's going on?

0119

This deviates from the format, it should be *119**.

The next consecutive twenty-five (30) playbacks

Twenty five does not equal 30.

At playback 159

159 is not between 170 - 200.

Until someone fixes these, I have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And yet I have no idea why I downvoted. That has been amended. I think this stands on its own alongside other recording-based SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This may be a bit of a necro-post (and please tell me if so, I'm new here) but the numbering at the end of the log seems to overlap or not line up with what is in text. I was wondering if these were on purpose or not, specifically that the numbering goes 078, 0119, 122

138 | The next consecutive twenty-five (30) playbacks follow SCP-1614-1 alone…

It also goes from 138 to 170 before switching point of view to SCP-1614-2, but then the entry for 170-200 reads

170 - 200 | At playback 159 point of view shifts to SCP-1614-2

Which is lower than the previous entry.

Aside from that, I really liked this SCP. I love how you've taken it past the point of boredom and shown how the experiences affected the relationship (a la Archonoid). Also, it feels like there should be more tests, but that's a great thing because it makes me want more. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"but have since gone on to include dancing"

so deviant. jokes aside this just seems to lack something i cant describe. it feels like the logs shouldnt end, or havent ended on enough of a cool thing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Padded out the video log to make it a little more unique than "abooga wooga evil digital people" and cleaned up the main article a little. Thanks for the crits guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fishhook hanging part was good, the murdering random people part is kinda bad. The jumping, murdering, photoshoots, etc. all make sense to be part of their experiment to be closer to each other, that I understand. The mass murdering part… dunno, seems kinda tacked on for the sake of making it a "oh scip go crazy kill people" thing.

I like it, kinda reminds me of a painting I saw before which is basically a picnic with 2 lovers, but the lovers are turned into the food they are eating so essentially they are eating each other. Forgot the name of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would be interesting and possibly reverse my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How about if the couple is portrayed in the process of using a documented SCP? The only horrifying example I can think of is a video of them writing about SCP-582. Surely something like that would kill testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd also like to see the turnaround back to mundane things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a combination of enough video SCPs (aside from 1733, I recall one wherein a family is seen doing different things every time it's viewed) that it could stand alone, especially given its status as an intentional project rather than random happenstance. However, there are far too many tone-breaking qualifiers ("depraved", "lurid") in the description for me to want to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It reminds me of 1733, yes, but it has all the aspects that made it interesting. My objection is to the last log. Why wouldn't the researchers want to test it more? You should have put there something more horrifying than murdering virtual people.

+0,99

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's on a flash drive inside a locker now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Please do not hotlink offsite. We have on-site image storage for a reason.

I am downvoting this one major issue: Why the hell would we put an anomalous file on a network-accessible server when the containment procedures specifically state that it's to be kept isolated? Not only is this inconsistent, it's incredibly stupid. Anything that's networked can be hacked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Secure networks = not secure. Flashdrive locked in a safe class locker at site 16 = secure. That's what they'd most likely do with it (in my opinion at least)

*In item 2 of the log, it describes the couple "fornicating," which means having premarital/nonmarital sex. Maybe try "engaging in coitus" or something equally uncomfortable and cold sounding like that.

*The article seems to focus a lot on the instantaneous appearance of physical items within the video. If it's a video, I think everyone kind of takes it for granted that we don't see where everything comes from. You could maybe put something about the "offscreen materialization of items necessary to perform activities"

I think I like it, but it weirds me out just enough to earn the upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this doesn't plummet come morning I will do all of this because I agree with everything you said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tentative +1 because I like the idea. However, I would like to see more out of this, especially more playback logs. The descent into depravity at the end feels rushed, perhaps bridge the gap by adding a more detailed series of events throughout the whole thing. Also, the suspension of testing indefinitely at the end is what links the article to 1733 the most, I would say. Maybe have the couples' actions become so horrifying eventually that testing is halted due to gross psychological trauma of the viewer, perhaps? Then you could get much more creative with your expungements at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: right in the feels

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is that a joke about The Flea by John Donne? If it is I hate you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As Scantron has said, this has been done before more successfully on another SCP object. I don't feel like this one brings anything new or interesting to the table. As much as I love messed up intimacy like this, it doesn't actually go into any of that as well, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of agree with this. There's a fairly strong trope of (bored + immortal = depravity), which makes the ending a bit predictable. Of course, tropes are not inherently bad. 1733 also uses that trope, and it does it quite well.

Just as something to consider if you do want to change it: what if that's not a necessary outcome? You could do that in a few different ways. One is to simply not have the couple go into the flesh-rending, crime-committing direction. There are other aspects of "immortal shared experience" that could be investigated; you touch, for example, on a shared body language being developed - this could become manifest in a full-fledged synchronicity of actions. Perhaps even a blurring of separate identities into a psuedo-hive-mind.

Another option would be to go through all the body horror stuff - then come out the other side, and go back to picnics in the park. Are there any interesting implications to a couple being able to do all those profoundly bizarre things and then go back to "normal" experiences?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interesting concept, but "if you keep doing it things will get really nasty" is a really well-worn twist, especially since SCP-1733 does that in a non-forced, creepy way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Just barely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks ihpkmn, fixed that. As for this being a poor man's 1733, I wasn't trying to make lightning strike twice. The germ of this idea was, how profoundly would relationships change if we had virtually unlimited resources and time to explore every nuance of ourselves and another person? Unfortunately the only way I could think of exploring that was a digital environment like 1733. Either way, sink or swim, it was a fun SCP to write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
these are voluntary and want it to continue. I think that's kind of an interesting take on the idea

I agree with this. I liked the descriptions of the couple and the pre-078 playbacks, but the out-of-the-blue tone change and body horror ending didn't really do much for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see two major differences between this and 1733.

One, there's an implication that they might actually be influencing something outside the videotape. I'd kind of like this to be more clear - are they really murdering people? Or is it all just images? "The Foundation doesn't know" is a valid answer, but if that's the case, it seems like it should be more explicit.

Two, 1733 is involuntary and wants it to stop; these are voluntary and want it to continue. I think that's kind of an interesting take on the idea, although I'm not sure if it's enough of a twist to justify a separate article.

There is of course a natural question: if both exist in the same universe, are they manifestations of the same underlying anomaly? Obviously, this should not actually be answered in the text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but I'm noticing an error or two with the numbering of the playbacks.

055 SCP-1614-1 and -2 do not visibly move or speak for the duration of Playback 049.

024 Playback 009

That's just a bit of an error, though, nothing to really detract from this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was liking this until the ending. Ending on sudden body horror, seemingly just for the sake of body horror, wasn't very satisfying. It also read like a lacklustre version of your SCP-1733.
